title: Compare area and perimeter
description: Compare the areas and perimeters of rectangles when given a context or picture.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Do the following pictures have the same perimeter, area, or both?**

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
We can find perimeter by adding all of the side lengths:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD6  + \blueD6 + \blueD6  + \blueD6 = \blueD{24}\text{ in}$


[[☃ image 2]]

   $\greenD5  + \greenD5  + \greenD7  + \greenD7  = \greenD{24}\text{ in}$

$\blueD{24}$ inches $=$ $\greenD{24}$ inches  
We can find area by multiplying the side lengths:  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD 6 \times \blueD6  = \blueD{36}\text{ sq in}$

[[☃ image 2]]

$\greenD5  \times \greenD7  = \greenD{35}\text{ sq in}$  

$\blueD{36}$ square inches $>\greenD{35}$ square inches
The pictures have the same perimeter only.

options.alt: Two framed pictures. The square picture of a bird has two sides labeled. The right side is labeled 6 inches.  The bottom side is labeled 6 inches. The rectangle picture of flowers also has two sides labeled. The right side is labeled 5 inches. The bottom side is labeled 7 inches.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Do the following pictures have the same perimeter, area, or both?**

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
Perimeter can be found by adding all of the side lengths:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD5  + \blueD5 + \blueD5  + \blueD5 = \blueD{20}\text{ in}$

[[☃ image 2]]

 $\greenD4  + \greenD4  + \greenD6  + \greenD6  = \greenD{20}\text{ in}$

$\blueD{20}$ inches $=$ $\greenD{20}$ inches  
Area can be found by multiplying the side lengths:  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD5 \times \blueD5  = \blueD{25}\text{ sq in}$

[[☃ image 2]]

$\greenD4  \times \greenD6  = \greenD{24}\text{ sq in}$  

$\blueD{25}$ square inches $>\greenD{24}$ square inches
The pictures have the same perimeter only.

options.alt: Two framed pictures. The square picture of flowers has two sides labeled. The right side is labeled 5 inches.  The bottom side is labeled 5 inches. The rectangle picture of clouds also has two sides labeled. The right side is labeled 4 inches. The bottom side is labeled 6 inches.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Do the following cards have the same perimeter, area, or both?**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ image 2]]

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
Perimeter can be found by adding all of the side lengths:




[[☃ image 1]]

  

$\blueD8  + \blueD8+ \blueD{10}  + \blueD{10} = \blueD{36}\text{ cm}$



[[☃ image 2]]

$\greenD{10}  + \greenD{10}  + \greenD8  + \greenD8  = \greenD{36}\text{ cm}$

$\blueD{36}$ centimeters $=$ $\greenD{36}$ centimeters  
Area can be found by multiplying the side lengths:  




[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD8 \times \blueD{10}  = \blueD{80}\text{ sq cm}$



[[☃ image 2]]

$\greenD{10}  \times \greenD8  = \greenD{80}\text{ sq cm}$  

$\blueD{80}$ square centimeters $=\greenD{80}$ square centimeters
The cards have the same perimeter and same area.

options.alt: A rectangular card labeled Happy Birthday. The top side of the card is labeled 8 centimeters. The left side of the card is labeled 10 centimeters.
A rectangular card labeled Thank you. The top side of the card is labeled 10 centimeters. The left side of the card is labeled 8 centimeters.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Do the following bulletin boards have the same perimeter, area, or both?** 

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
Perimeter can be found by adding all of the side lengths:

[[☃ image 1]]

 $\blueD3  + \blueD3 + \blueD4  + \blueD4 = \blueD{14}\text{ ft}$

[[☃ image 2]]

$\greenD4  + \greenD4  + \greenD3  + \greenD3 = \greenD{14}\text{ ft}$

$\blueD{14}$ feet $=$ $\greenD{14}$ feet

Area can be found by multiplying the side lengths:  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD 3 \times \blueD4  = \blueD{12}\text{ sq ft}$

[[☃ image 2]]

$\greenD4  \times \greenD3  = \greenD{12}\text{ sq ft}$  

$\blueD{12}$ square feet $=\greenD{12}$ square feet
The bulletin boards have the same perimeter and same area.

options.alt: Two bulletin boards. A tall bulletin board with stars on it measures 3 feet along the bottom edge and 4 feet along its right edge. A wide bulletin board with circles on it measures 4 feet along the bottom edge and 3 feet along its right edge.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Do the following sports fields have the same perimeter, area, or both?**

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
Perimeter can be found by adding all of the side lengths:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\greenD3  + \greenD3  + \greenD8  + \greenD8  = \greenD{22}\text{ m}$

[[☃ image 2]]

$\blueD4  + \blueD4 + \blueD6 + \blueD6 = \blueD{20}\text{ m}$

$\greenD{22}$ meters $>$ $\blueD{20}$ meters

Area can be found by multiplying the side lengths:  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\greenD3  \times \greenD8  = \greenD{24}\text{ sq m}$

[[☃ image 2]]

$\blueD4  \times \blueD6  = \blueD{24}\text{ sq m}$  

$\greenD{24}$ square meters $=\blueD{24}$ square meters
The fields have the same area only.

options.alt: Two tall rectangles. A tall green rectangle labeled Soccer. The rectangle is labeled 3 meters along the bottom. Along the right side, it is labeled 8 meters. A tall blue rectangle, shorter and wider than the green one, is labeled Tennis. Along the bottom, the rectangle is labeled 4 meters. Along the right side, it is labeled 6 meters.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Do the following sports fields have the same perimeter, area, or both?**

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
Perimeter can be found by adding all of the side lengths:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD4  + \blueD4 + \blueD7 + \blueD7 = \blueD{22}\text{ m}$

[[☃ image 2]]

$\greenD3  + \greenD3  + \greenD8  + \greenD8  = \greenD{22}\text{ m}$

$\blueD{22}$ meters $=$ $\greenD{22}$ meters

Area can be found by multiplying the side lengths:  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD4  \times \blueD7  = \blueD{28}\text{ sq m}$

[[☃ image 2]]

$\greenD3  \times \greenD8  = \greenD{24}\text{ sq m}$  

$\blueD{28}$ square meters $>\greenD{24}$ square meters
The fields have the same perimeter only.

options.alt: Two tall rectangles. A tall blue rectangle is labeled with a tennis ball. The rectangle is labeled 4 meters along the bottom. Along the right side, it is labeled 7 meters. A brown rectangle, taller and narrower than the blue one, is labeled with a basketball. Along the bottom, the rectangle is labeled 3 meters. Along the right side, it is labeled 8 meters.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Do the following sports fields have the same perimeter, area, or both?**



[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
Perimeter can be found by adding all of the side lengths:



[[☃ image 1]]

 

$\greenD6  + \greenD6  + \greenD6  + \greenD6  = \greenD{24}\text{ m}$



[[☃ image 2]]

$\blueD4  + \blueD4 + \blueD9 + \blueD9 = \blueD{26}\text{ m}$

$\greenD{24}$ meters $<$ $\blueD{26}$ meters

Area can be found by multiplying the side lengths:  



[[☃ image 1]]

$\greenD6  \times \greenD6  = \greenD{36}\text{ sq m}$


[[☃ image 2]]

$\blueD4  \times \blueD9  = \blueD{36}\text{ sq m}$  

$\greenD{36}$ square meters $=\blueD{36}$ square meters
The fields have the same area only.

options.alt: A square and a rectangle. The square is labeled with a baseball. The right side of the square is labeled 6 meters. The bottom of the square is labeled 6 meters. The rectangle  is labeled with a basketball. The right side of the rectangle is labeled 9 meters. The bottom of the rectangle is labeled 4 meters.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Do the following notebooks have the same perimeter, area, or both?**

[[☃ image 1]]

[[☃ radio 3]]
hints:
Perimeter can be found by adding all of the side lengths:

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD4  + \blueD4 + \blueD{10}  + \blueD{10} = \blueD{28}\text{ in}$

[[☃ image 2]]

$\greenD5  + \greenD5  + \greenD8  + \greenD8 = \greenD{26}\text{ in}$

$\blueD{28}$ inches $>$ $\greenD{26}$ inches

Area can be found by multiplying the side lengths:  

[[☃ image 1]]

$\blueD 4 \times \blueD{10}  = \blueD{40}\text{ sq in}$

[[☃ image 2]]

$\greenD5  \times \greenD8 = \greenD{40}\text{ sq in}$  

$\blueD{40}$ square inches $=\greenD{40}$ square inches
The notebooks have the same area only.

options.alt: Two notebooks shaped like rectangles. The first rectangular notebook is taller and narrower than the second notebook.  It has apples on the cover. The second notebook has a flower on the cover. The bottom of the first notebook is labeled 4 inches and the right side is labeled 10 inches. On the second notebook, the bottom edge is labeled 5 inches and the right edge is labeled 8 inches.

**Do the following blankets have the same perimeter, area, or both?**

hints:

$\blueD3  + \blueD3 + \blueD3  + \blueD3 = \blueD{12}\text{ m}$



[[☃ image 2]]

 

$\greenD4  + \greenD4  + \greenD2  + \greenD2  = \greenD{12}\text{ m}$

$\blueD{12}$ meters $=$ $\greenD{12}$ meters

options.alt: Two blankets. The first blanket is shaped like a square. The bottom of the first blanket is labeled 3 meters and the right side is labeled 3 meters. The second blanket is shaped like a rectangle. The bottom edge is labeled 2 meters and the right edge is labeled 4 meters.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Do the following rectangles have the same perimeter, area, or both?**


[[☃ image 1]]


[[☃ image 2]]

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
First, let's find the area and perimeter of the striped rectangle.  


[[☃ image 1]]

We can find area of a rectangle by multiplying its length by its width.  
Area $= \greenD8 \times \greenD5 = \greenD{40} \text{ sq cm}$ 

We find perimeter by adding the lengths of the sides.  
Perimeter $= \greenD8 + \greenD8 + \greenD5 + \greenD5 = \greenD{26} \text{ cm}$

Next, we find the area and perimeter of the other rectangle. 

$$ | Area | Perimeter
:- | :- | :-
![A wide rectangle. The left side of the rectangle is labeled 5 centimeters. The top side of the rectangle is labeled 8 centimeters.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/bd19da107301399d2d2407015d1c49c75d6b8282) | $\greenD8 \!\times\!\! \greenD5 \!=\!\! \greenD{40} \text{ sq cm}$  | $\greenD8 \!+\!\! \greenD8 \!+\!\! \greenD5 \!+\!\! \greenD5 \!=\!\! \greenD{26} \text{ cm}$
![A tall rectangle. The left side of the rectangle is labeled 10 centimeters. The top side of the rectangle is labeled 3 centimeters.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2362d1c42eb35a10bdeaf67dabe2abeba374682f) | $\blueD3 \!\times\!\!\! \blueD{10} \!=\!\! \blueD{30} \text{ sq cm}$   | $\blueD3 \!+\!\! \blueD3\!+\!\!\! \blueD{10} \!+\!\!\! \blueD{10} \!=\! \!\blueD{26} \text{ cm}$

Now, lets compare the area and perimeters of the rectangles.

Area:  
$\greenD{40}\text { sq cm}\neq\blueD{30}\text{ sq cm}$

Perimeter:  
$\greenD{26}\text { cm}=\blueD{26}\text{ cm}$
The rectangles have the same perimeter only.

options.alt: A wide rectangle. The left side of the rectangle is labeled 5 centimeters. The top side of the rectangle is labeled 8 centimeters.
A tall rectangle. The left side of the rectangle is labeled 10 centimeters. The top side of the rectangle is labeled 3 centimeters.

hints:
First, let's find the area and perimeter of the striped rectangle.  

[[☃ image 1]]

We can find area of a rectangle by multiplying its length by its width.  
Area $= \greenD4 \times \greenD6 = \greenD{24} \text{ sq cm}$ 

We find perimeter by adding the lengths of the sides.  
Perimeter $= \greenD4 + \greenD4 + \greenD6 + \greenD6 = \greenD{20} \text{ cm}$

Next, we find the area and perimeter of the other rectangle. 

$$ | Area | Perimeter
:- | :- | :-
![A wide rectangle. The left side of the rectangle is labeled 4 centimeters. The top side of the rectangle is labeled 6 centimeters.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1422cf4afc6f10d900b5968ccb674504d47a3081) | $\greenD4 \!\times\!\! \greenD6 \!=\!\! \greenD{24} \text{ sq cm}$  | $\greenD4 \!+\!\! \greenD4 \!+\!\! \greenD6 \!+\!\! \greenD6 \!=\!\! \greenD{20} \text{ cm}$
![A square. The left side of the square is labeled 5 centimeters. The top side of the square is labeled 5 centimeters.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/87ac26c585a552f7beb2cb68c07bde45c39516d6) | $\purpleD5 \times \purpleD{5} = \purpleD{25} \text{ sq cm}$   | $\purpleD5 + \purpleD5 + \purpleD{5} + \purpleD{5} = \purpleD{20} \text{ cm}$

Now, lets compare the area and perimeters of the rectangles.

Area:  
$\greenD{24}\text { sq cm}\neq\purpleD{25}\text{ sq cm}$

Perimeter:  
$\greenD{20}\text { cm}=\purpleD{20}\text{ cm}$
The rectangles have the same perimeter only.

options.alt: A rectangle. The left side of the rectangle is labeled 4 centimeters. The top side of the rectangle is labeled 6 centimeters.
A square. The left side of the square is labeled 5 centimeters. The top side of the square is labeled 5 centimeters.

hints:
First, let's find the area and perimeter of the striped rectangle.  

[[☃ image 1]]

We can find area of a rectangle by multiplying its length by its width.  
Area $= \greenD{3} \times \greenD{4} \!=\!\! \greenD{12} \text{ sq cm}$ 

We find perimeter by adding the lengths of the sides.  
Perimeter $= \greenD3 + \greenD3 + \greenD{4} + \greenD{4} = \greenD{14} \text{ cm}$

Next, we find the area and perimeter of the other rectangle. 

$$ | Area | Perimeter
:- | :- | :-
